1: A type: Psalm 38 is one of seven Penitential Psalms (Pss 6, 32, 38, 51, 102, 130, 143) and is designated a memorial offering (lĕhazkîr — possibly the frankincense portion of the grain offering, Leviticus 2:2; or a memorial to bring a matter before God). "LORD, do not rebuke me in your anger" opens the psalm with an appeal for measured discipline rather than fierce wrath. The typological reading that Hebrews and the NT invite: what David experiences as divine discipline for personal sin, Christ undergoes for the sin of others — he takes the rebuke that was ours (Isaiah 53:5: "the punishment that brought us peace was on him").
2: A type: "Your arrows have pierced me deeply, and your hand has come down hard on me." The divine arrows and pressing hand are the imagery of YHWH's judicial wrath falling on the person. Isaiah 53:4 states: "We considered him punished by God, stricken by him, and afflicted." What David describes as his own experience of divine discipline under sin, the NT applies to Christ bearing the judgment that sin requires — the arrows of divine wrath directed at David's sin fall on the one who takes his place. 1 Peter 2:24: "He himself bore our sins in his body on the cross."
3: A type: "My body has no soundness because of your anger; my bones have no rest because of my sin." The physical disintegration produced by the divine anger over sin is the imagery of judgment impacting the body. At the crucifixion, the physical suffering of Christ — the body broken — fulfills this imagery not through personal sin but as the sin-bearer. Psalms 22:14-17 uses similar language for Christ's crucifixion experience. The "no soundness" (ʾên-šālôm) in David's body under divine anger over sin becomes the literally broken body of Christ under the sin of others.
4: A type: "My iniquities have overwhelmed me — they are a burden too heavy to carry." The crushing weight of accumulated sin — iniquities that overwhelm and cannot be borne — is the human condition under covenant judgment. Isaiah 53:6: "the LORD has laid on him the iniquity of us all." What the Psalmist describes as the unbearable weight of his own sin, the Servant song frames as the weight of collective human sin laid on the Servant. Christ is the one who actually carries what no individual can bear: the full weight of human iniquity.
5: A type: "My wounds stink and fester because of my own foolishness." The festering, malodorous wounds of the Psalmist under divine judgment for sin are the imagery of spiritual-physical degradation. The wounds of Christ at the crucifixion — the lash marks, the nail wounds, the spear — are in the NT the wounds of healing, not of personal sin: 1 Peter 2:24, "by his wounds you have been healed." The festering wound-imagery of Psalms 38:5 becomes, in Christ, the healing wound: the wound of the sin-bearer that heals those whose sin caused it.
6: A type: "I am bowed low and hunched over; I walk about mourning all day long." The physical posture of mourning under divine judgment — bowed, bent, unable to stand upright — is the image of the penitent under the weight of sin. Isaiah 53:3 describes the Servant as "a man of sorrows, and familiar with pain" who was "despised." Christ in Gethsemane "fell with his face to the ground" (Matthew 26:39) — the same posture of the overwhelmed under the weight of what he was to bear. The hunched mourning of Psalms 38:6 is the incarnate Messiah's posture before his cross.
7: A type: "My loins are on fire with disease, and there is no health anywhere in my body." The comprehensive bodily affliction — fire, disease, no health — is the Psalm's extreme expression of being under divine judgment for sin. The physical totality of Christ's suffering at the crucifixion — the crown of thorns, the flogging, the cross — embodied this "no health anywhere." But more profoundly: 2 Corinthians 5:21, "God made him who had no sin to be sin for us." The one who had no personal "disease" was made to carry all of ours.
8: A type: "I am benumbed and utterly crushed; I cry out from the anguish of my heart." The numbness, crushing, and heart-anguish are the terminal point of the Psalm's emotional and physical description of the one under sin-judgment. In Gethsemane: "My soul is overwhelmed with sorrow to the point of death" (Matthew 26:38). And on the cross: "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" (Matthew 27:46, citing Psalms 22:1). The "anguish of the heart" from which David cries is the type of the agony from which Christ cries — and the cry is heard (Hebrews 5:7).
9: A revelation of God: "Lord, all my longing is laid out before you; my groaning is not hidden from you." The divine omniscience of the sufferer's inner state — nothing hidden, all groaning known — is the ground of prayer's confidence. Hebrews 4:13: "Nothing in all creation is hidden from God's sight. Everything is uncovered and laid bare before the eyes of him to whom we must give account." Christ as the great high priest (Hebrews 4:14-16) brings the sufferer's groaning directly before the divine throne — the one who himself groaned (John 11:33, 38; Romans 8:23) now intercedes for those who groan.
10: A type: "My heart pounds; my strength has failed, and even the light of my eyes has deserted me." The catalogue of physical and vital-force failures — heart, strength, sight all giving way — describes the one who is at death's door under divine judgment. The Passion narratives record Christ's physical exhaustion (he could not carry the cross, Luke 23:26), his cry of dereliction (Matthew 27:46), and his death (John 19:30: "It is finished. And he bowed his head and gave up his spirit"). The failing of all vital forces in Psalms 38:10 is the type of Christ's physical death on the cross.
11: A direct connection: "My friends and companions keep away from my affliction, and even my relatives stand at a distance." This verse finds a near-verbatim fulfillment in Luke 23:49: "But all those who knew him, including the women who had followed him from Galilee, stood at a distance, watching these things." The disciples fled at the arrest (Mark 14:50), and those who remained stood at a distance at the cross — exactly the scenario Psalms 38:11 describes. What David experienced in personal affliction, Christ experienced as the culminating moment of his Passion.
12: A shadow: "Those who want me dead set traps for me; those who seek to harm me speak of my destruction and scheme all day long." The constant scheming by those who seek the righteous person's death — traps set, destruction planned — is the Passion narrative's background. John 11:53 ("they plotted to take his life"), Matthew 26:3-5 (the assembly plotting to kill Jesus), and Psalms 2:1-2 (the nations conspiring against YHWH's anointed) all converge in the Sanhedrin's deliberate plan to execute the righteous Messiah.
13: A type: "But I am like someone who cannot hear — I do not listen; like someone mute who cannot speak." The deliberate silence of the Psalmist before his accusers — not engaging with their attacks — is the type of Christ's silence before the Sanhedrin and Pilate. Matthew 26:63: "But Jesus remained silent." Matthew 27:14: "he gave him no answer, not even to a single charge — to the great amazement of the governor." Isaiah 53:7: "He was oppressed and afflicted, yet he did not open his mouth; he was led like a lamb to the slaughter, and as a sheep before its shearers is silent, so he did not open his mouth." Psalms 38:13 is the type; Isaiah 53:7 is the prophecy; the Passion is the fulfillment.
14: A type: "I am like a man who does not hear, with no counter-arguments in his mouth." The complete absence of self-defense — no counter-arguments, no refutation — is the posture of the one who entrusts the verdict to God rather than human judgment. 1 Peter 2:23: "When they hurled their insults at him, he did not retaliate; when he suffered, he made no threats. Instead, he entrusted himself to him who judges justly." Christ's silence in the trial is not helplessness — it is the total entrusting of his cause to the Father who vindicated him in the resurrection (the divine counter-argument to the human verdict).
15: A revelation of God: "But in you, LORD, I place my hope; you will answer, O Lord my God." The turn from affliction to hope — the pivot of all the Penitential Psalms — is possible because YHWH is the answerer. Hebrews 5:7: "During the days of Jesus's life on earth, he offered up prayers and petitions with fervent cries and tears to the one who could save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverent submission." Christ's hope in the Father ("you will answer") was vindicated: the Father answered the crucified Son's prayer by raising him from the dead.
16: A theme: "For I thought: Do not let them gloat over me — those who triumph over me when I stumble." The concern about the enemy's gloating triumph is the honor dimension of the Psalm: the sufferer does not want his defeat to become the occasion for the wicked's celebration. The mockery at the cross (Matthew 27:39-44: "Come down from the cross... He trusts in God. Let God rescue him now") is exactly the gloating triumph the Psalmist fears. The divine answer — the resurrection — vindicates the one who was mocked and ensures that the gloating is ultimately silenced.
17: A type: "For I am about to stumble and my pain is always with me." The constant pain and imminent stumbling — the sufferer at the edge of collapse — is the threshold state that the Passion pushes through. Isaiah 53:3-4 describes the Servant as "familiar with pain." In Gethsemane, the pain is so acute that Christ's sweat is like drops of blood (Luke 22:44). The "always with me" of constant pain is the Incarnation's acceptance of the full weight of human suffering, carried voluntarily by the one who "became flesh" (John 1:14).
18: A type: "I confess my wrongdoing; I am distressed by my sin." The Penitential Psalm's confession of sin — the Psalmist's own genuine guilt before God — is the framework within which the typological reading operates carefully: Christ has no wrongdoing to confess (2 Corinthians 5:21; Hebrews 4:15). Yet he enters fully into the position of the one under divine judgment for sin. 2 Corinthians 5:21: "God made him who had no sin to be sin for us, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God." The confessor of Psalms 38:18 and the sinless Christ of the NT are not identical — but one is the type and the other the antitype who takes the confession's burden.
19: A theme: "But my enemies are full of life and strong; those who hate me without cause have multiplied." The vitality of the enemies and the baselessness of their hatred ("without cause") is the injustice theme of the Passion. John 15:25 quotes Psalms 69:4 — "they hated me without reason" — as a direct reference to those who opposed Jesus. The "without cause" hatred of Psalms 38:19 is the hatred of those who oppose the righteous because they are righteous, not because of any offense — the condition Christ experienced throughout his ministry.
20: A type: "Those who repay good with evil oppose me, because I pursue what is right." The inversion of just recompense — evil returned for good — is the shape of the Passion. Christ "went around doing good" (Acts 10:38) and was executed for it. Romans 5:8: "God demonstrates his own love for us in this: While we were still sinners, Christ died for us." Those who repay the greatest good (Christ's redemptive mission) with the greatest evil (crucifixion) are the ultimate fulfillment of Psalms 38:20's picture of the one opposed for pursuing righteousness.
21: A theme: "Do not abandon me, LORD; my God, do not stay far away." The prayer against divine abandonment — the deepest fear of the covenant person under judgment — reaches its ultimate expression in Christ's cry on the cross: "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" (Matthew 27:46, Psalms 22:1). The prayer "do not abandon me" becomes the question "why have you forsaken me?" — not because the Father actually abandons the Son permanently, but because the sin-bearing Messiah enters the full experience of separation that sin produces. The resurrection is the Father's answer: "I have not abandoned you."
22: A revelation of God: "Come quickly to help me, O Lord, my salvation." The final urgent cry — speed of divine help, divine identity as "my salvation" (yĕšûʿātî) — completes the Penitential Psalm with a prayer rather than an answer. The prayer for quick divine rescue is answered definitively in the resurrection: "in just a little while, he who is coming will come and will not delay" (Hebrews 10:37). The "Lord, my salvation" (YHWH yĕšûʿātî) is the name that the NT gives to Jesus — Yēšûaʿ means "YHWH saves." The Lord who is salvation is the name given to the one who answers the Psalm's final cry.
